result.concurrencyAngle: Two-phase commit for multi-seat atomicity under a per-showtime ReentrantLock: holdSeats first CHECKS all requested seats are AVAILABLE (phase 1, collecting conflicts), then HOLDS all or throws SeatsUnavailableException having touched nothing (phase 2). The lock is realized via Template Method hooks (gateLock/gateUnlock are no-ops in the clean BookingService, overridden as a ConcurrentHashMap of per-showtime ReentrantLocks via atomic computeIfAbsent in ThreadSafeBookingService). Hold expiry: 10-min window with an injected Clock, reclaimed by sweepExpiredHolds() (background-sweep style) plus a confirm-time isExpired guard so a late payment can never sneak through. Concurrency test: 50 threads race the same overlapping seat pair behind a CyclicBarrier, asserting exactly 1 winner, 49 typed SeatsUnavailableException losses, and post-state of exactly 2 HELD seats and 1 live hold; a second test proves disjoint requests all succeed in parallel.

Lock striping: ConcurrentHashMap<String, CustomerBucket> where each CustomerBucket owns its own ReentrantLock, so requests from different customers never contend and only same-customer requests serialize on the shared token count. The lock is wired via Template Method hooks acquireGate/releaseGate that are no-ops in the clean base RateLimiterService and overridden in the 6-line ThreadSafeRateLimiterService to lock/unlock the per-customer lock. Clock is injected (Clock interface, SystemClock/FakeClock) for sleep-free refill tests. Strategy pattern: TokenBucketStrategy (lazy refill) and SlidingWindowLogStrategy behind RateLimitStrategy, built by StrategyFactory. Two concurrency tests: 50 threads hammer ONE customer at the boundary asserting exactly the limit allowed (10 allowed, 40 denied, 0 errors); 40 customers each on their own thread each get exactly their own limit, proving no cross-contention. CyclicBarrier forces a true simultaneous stampede.

Per-spot fine-grained ReentrantLock. Each ParkingSpot carries its own lock; park() acquires that spot's lock via the Template Method withSpotGuard hook (no-op in the clean ParkingLotService, overridden to lock in ThreadSafeParkingLotService), then re-checks isFree inside the lock before occupy — closing the check-then-act double-allocation race. Two cars going to different spots take different locks so they park fully in parallel (no global bottleneck). Proven by a 50-thread CyclicBarrier stampede over 5 spots asserting exactly 5 winners on 5 DISTINCT spots, 45 typed NoSpotAvailableException, 0 surprise exceptions, plus a 20-thread park/unpark churn test ending with all spots FREE.

findings.problem: Stranded-seat LEAK on duplicate seat labels — the exact failure the two-phase commit claims to prevent. A request like holdSeats("show-1", ["A1","A1"]) passes phase 1 (isAvailable() returns true for A1 twice), then phase 2 calls seat.hold() on A1 (flips it to HELD), then calls hold() on A1 AGAIN, which throws InvalidBookingException("Cannot hold seat A1 in status HELD") from inside ShowSeat. holdSeats only anticipates SeatsUnavailableException, so this throw propagates uncaught: A1 is left HELD, NO hold is recorded in the registry. Verified empirically: after the throw, HELD=1 but liveHoldCount=0, and advancing the clock 30 min + sweepExpiredHolds() releases 0 — the seat is PERMANENTLY stranded (no hold id exists to cancel/confirm/sweep). This directly falsifies the writeup's headline claim (§5.5 D: "every seat passed, so this cannot fail midway" / "no stranded seats"). Happens identically under the per-showtime lock in ThreadSafeBookingService.
findings.fix: Deduplicate/validate the seat-label set before phase 2 (e.g. reject duplicates in validateSeatRequest with InvalidBookingException, or build resolveSeats over a LinkedHashSet). Alternatively wrap phase 2 so any hold() failure rolls back already-held seats in this request.

findings.problem: §6 incompleteness: the skeleton marks cancelHold and sweepExpiredHolds with `/* §6 below */`, but their full Java bodies are NEVER written in §6 — only pseudocode appears in §5.5. Additionally, resolveSeats(...) is CALLED in the §6 holdSeats code (lines 820, 1131) but is never defined anywhere in the writeup (the Helper methods block defines only requireShowtime, requireShowtimeIdForHold, validateSeatRequest, releaseHold). liveHoldCount() exists on disk and is used by the tests but is absent from the writeup. No semantic drift in the code that IS shown — holdSeats/confirmBooking/helpers match disk — this is a doc-completeness gap, not a code defect.
findings.fix: Add the full Java bodies for cancelHold and sweepExpiredHolds to §6 (they are promised), and include the resolveSeats helper definition so every method referenced in §6 is shown.

findings.problem: Checklist item '§4.1 every class ends with COMPLETE java block' is not satisfied: §4.1 contains ZERO Java code fences. Every per-class derivation block uses UML/pseudocode notation (`class BookingService:` with `+ method() -> Type`), not compilable Java. The complete Java only appears in §6. If the rubric requires a complete Java block to close each class in §4.1, this format deviates.
findings.fix: If the rubric requires it, append a complete Java block to each §4.1 class derivation (or relocate §6's Java under each §4.1 class).

findings.problem: No actual background thread is wired. The writeup repeatedly states a "timer thread calls this every minute" (§2 lists a "Background sweeper — a timer thread"; BookingService.java:176 comment says "A timer thread calls this every minute"), but sweepExpiredHolds() is only ever invoked manually by tests/Main. The required angle asks for a "background thread [that] scans held seats older than 10min." The sweep LOGIC is correct and verified, but the scheduling claimed in prose is not implemented.
findings.fix: Either wire a ScheduledExecutorService (daemon) that calls sweepExpiredHolds() on an interval and expose start/stop, or soften the prose to state the sweep is the unit of work invoked by an external scheduler.

findings.problem: The 'disjoint succeed in parallel' test does not actually prove the per-showtime lock avoids needless serialization (the property the writeup brags about). 4 threads each hold a single distinct seat; this assertion (all 4 win, 4 HELD) passes equally well under ONE global lock or even a fully serial implementation — it never measures parallelism or contention. It is not a strong test of the granularity claim.
findings.fix: To actually demonstrate non-serialization, test concurrent holds across MULTIPLE distinct showtimes and assert no false SeatsUnavailable conflicts, or measure that disjoint requests do not block each other (e.g. via timing/latch ordering).

findings.problem: Dead code: getHeldBy() and getHeldUntil() accessors are defined on ShowSeat but nothing in the codebase, tests, or Main reads them. They also are not present in the writeup, so they are unmentioned dead surface. Minor, but inflates the class beyond what the design narrates.
findings.fix: Remove the unused accessors, or use them in a state-inspection/diagnostic path if intended.

findings.problem: This is the test the prompt explicitly asks for ('threads across MANY customers prove no cross-contention') but it is a TRIVIAL test that cannot catch the bug it claims to. It spawns exactly ONE thread per customer (40 threads, 40 distinct customer ids). Each customer's bucket/strategy is therefore only ever touched by a single thread — there is zero concurrent access to any individual customer's state, so the per-customer lock is never exercised. I proved this empirically: I swapped the helper to construct the non-locking base RateLimiterService and ran this test 10x — it passed 10/10 (0/10 caught the missing lock). The CyclicBarrier only makes the 40 threads START together; since they never share a bucket, the start timing is irrelevant. The test name and the §9 Part C / §7.2 claims ('proving zero cross-contention', 'proving customer A's traffic never consumed customer B's budget') are therefore unsupported — the test would pass identically with or without any locking at all.
findings.fix: Make multiple threads hammer EACH customer concurrently (e.g. 40 customers x 5 threads each = 200 threads, all released on one barrier), then assert each customer got exactly its limit AND that interleaving actually occurred. Cross-contention is only detectable when (a) >1 thread contends per customer to exercise the lock, and (b) you separately assert a noisy customer running flat-out does not reduce a quiet customer's count. As written it proves nothing about contention.

findings.problem: The headline concurrency test is a weak/flaky detector of the very race it exists to guard. I mutated the helper to use the non-locking base RateLimiterService and ran this test 10x in isolation: it caught the race only 2/10 times (passed 8/10 despite the broken, unlocked implementation). With 50 threads released on a CyclicBarrier but a critical section that is a few nanoseconds of double arithmetic, the threads rarely actually overlap inside tryAcquire on a fast machine, so 'allowed==10' frequently holds even with NO lock. A test that lets a known-broken implementation pass ~80% of the time provides little protection against regressions and overstates the 'strong-hire' concurrency claim. The doc (§7.2, §9 Part C) presents these as strict proofs that exactly the limit is allowed under contention.
findings.fix: Strengthen so the race is reliably exposed when locking is absent: widen the critical section in test mode (e.g. a strategy that does a tiny sleep/yield between check and spend), or run many iterations and assert across all of them, or use a barrier-plus-spin to maximize true overlap. At minimum, add an assertion-backed test that the BASE (unlocked) service DOES over-allow, to lock in that the thread-safe subclass is what makes the difference.

findings.problem: Dead field: 'private final RateLimitPolicy policy;' is stored in the constructor but never read after construction — maxTokens and refillPerNano are derived from it in the ctor and the field is never referenced again. Minor Java hygiene/noise; the §4.1 state derivation table does not list 'policy' as state for this class, so the stored reference is unjustified.
findings.fix: Read policy.capacity()/policy.tokensPerNano() into locals in the constructor and drop the field, or document why the reference is retained.

findings.problem: The doc labels RateLimiterService o-- CustomerBucket as 'aggregation, not composition, because buckets are conceptually the customers' state the service tracks', yet in code the service exclusively creates and owns every CustomerBucket (newBucket() via computeIfAbsent), buckets have no independent lifecycle and are never injected or shared — that is composition, not aggregation. The CustomerBucket *-- RateLimitStrategy is correctly composition. This is a UML-semantics nit, not a code bug, but the stated justification is inconsistent with the implementation.
findings.fix: Either model service->bucket as composition (filled diamond) to match the code, or soften the prose; the current rationale contradicts the actual ownership.

findings.where: _response.md §9 — three locations: the 'Deadlock' row of the concurrency-vocabulary table ('avoided: we hold only ONE spot lock at a time'); the 'Why ReentrantLock' Q&A ('Today my call graph holds one spot lock at a time'); and the 'What's the deadlock risk?' Q&A ('a park holds exactly ONE spot lock and never grabs a second while holding it'). The code is ParkingLotService.park() lines 107-119.
findings.problem: The document's central deadlock-safety argument is factually FALSE and self-contradictory. park() does `return withSpotGuard(candidate, () -> { if (!candidate.isFree()) return park(vehicle); ... })` — i.e. when the re-check fails it calls park() RECURSIVELY while still holding candidate's lock, and the recursive call acquires a DIFFERENT spot's lock. I proved this empirically with an instrumented subclass under a 128-thread / 30k-iteration churn: a single thread holds up to 4 spot locks simultaneously (MAX simultaneous spot-locks held = 4). So the claims 'one spot lock at a time' and 'never grabs a second while holding it' are wrong. The doc even acknowledges the recursion in §5.5 ('the loser's re-check at step 5 sees OCCUPIED and recurses to L1-2') but never reconciles that with the deadlock table — the two statements directly contradict each other in the same document. An interviewer grading the per-spot-lock angle would immediately catch this: 'You told me you never hold two locks, but your retry recurses while holding the first lock.' Note also: the code does NOT actually deadlock (a held spot is always OCCUPIED, so the strategy never re-offers it → no AB-BA lock-ordering cycle can form; 200 stampede trials and the churn stress all passed clean), so this is a documentation/reasoning defect, not a live bug — hence MAJOR not BLOCKER.
findings.fix: Either (a) fix the narrative to tell the truth: 'the retry recurses while still holding the candidate's lock, so a thread can transiently hold several spot locks; this is still deadlock-free because a held spot is OCCUPIED and the strategy never re-offers an occupied spot, so no lock-ordering cycle can form' — and drop the false 'only ONE lock' / 'never grabs a second' lines; OR (b) change the code to not recurse under the lock: release the candidate's lock before retrying (e.g. return a sentinel from withSpotGuard and loop in park() OUTSIDE the guard), which makes the 'one lock at a time' claim actually true and also avoids holding the loser's lock during the entire retry.

findings.where: _response.md §9 'Why ReentrantLock' (and the Part-A code-block bullet 'Why ReentrantLock: a guarded method may call another guarded helper on the same spot; reentrant avoids a self-deadlock')
findings.problem: The stated justification for choosing ReentrantLock is the wrong reason. No guarded method calls another guarded method on the SAME spot anywhere in the code, so reentrancy as described is never exercised. The place reentrancy could plausibly matter — the recursive park() retry — actually acquires a DIFFERENT spot's lock, where reentrancy does not apply at all (it's plain nested locking of distinct locks). So the doc never correctly explains its own use of ReentrantLock; a plain lock would behave identically for the present call graph.
findings.fix: State the honest reason: ReentrantLock was chosen for its explicit try/finally unlock idiom and tryLock/timeout extensibility, not for re-entry (which the current call graph does not use). Or remove the 'guarded method calls another guarded method on the same spot' justification entirely.

findings.where: src/main/java/com/interview/lld/parking/service/ParkingLotService.java park() lines 110-112; same code mirrored in _response.md §6 and §9
findings.problem: The retry uses unbounded recursion (`return park(vehicle)`) instead of a bounded loop. Under sustained heavy contention each failed re-check adds a stack frame AND holds the parent spot's lock for the full duration of the retry, reducing the very concurrency the per-spot lock was meant to maximize (a loser keeps an unrelated spot's lock locked while it re-scans and re-locks another spot). In practice depth stayed small (max 4 observed) and no StackOverflow occurred in stress, so this is MINOR, but it is a real efficiency/robustness wart on the headline concurrency path.
findings.fix: Convert to an iterative retry loop that acquires the lock, checks, and on failure releases before re-running findSpot — bounded stack, no lock held across the re-scan.

result.summary:
Fixed the [MAJOR] stranded-seat leak on duplicate seat labels in java-lld-movieticket-001_lld by rejecting duplicates before the two-phase commit ever mutates state.

WHAT CHANGED (code):
- /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora_java/workspace/java-lld-movieticket-001_lld/src/main/java/com/interview/lld/moviebooking/service/BookingService.java
  - validateSeatRequest now tracks seen labels in a HashSet<String> and throws InvalidBookingException("Duplicate seat <label> in request") when a label repeats. Because validateSeatRequest runs BEFORE gateLock and before any seat is touched, the request backs out cleanly with no mutation — so a request like holdSeats("show-1", ["A1","A1"]) can never reach phase 2 and re-call seat.hold() on an already-HELD seat. This eliminates the uncaught InvalidBookingException-from-ShowSeat path that left A1 HELD with no hold recorded (HELD=1, liveHoldCount=0, unsweepable). Added imports java.util.HashSet and java.util.Set. Used HashSet (not LinkedHashSet) since order is irrelevant for a membership check. Fix is inherited unchanged by ThreadSafeBookingService (it only overrides gateLock/gateUnlock), so the per-showtime-lock path is fixed identically. Kept one base exception (InvalidBookingException extends BookingException), Java 17 idioms, no stub bodies.

WHAT CHANGED (test — strengthened, not weakened):
- /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora_java/workspace/java-lld-movieticket-001_lld/src/test/java/com/interview/lld/moviebooking/BookingServiceTest.java
  - Added regression test duplicateSeatLabelRejectedAndTouchesNothing: asserts the duplicate request throws InvalidBookingException, then asserts AVAILABLE=5, HELD=0, liveHoldCount=0 (no stranded seat), then proves A1 can still be held normally and that a real cancelable hold id exists afterward (the bug had none).

WHAT CHANGED (writeup §7 / §5):
- /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora_java/workspace/java-lld-movieticket-001_lld/_response.md
  - §7.2 updated test count 13 -> 14 in both the actual-output block and the prose, and noted the new duplicate-seat-label coverage.
  - §5 holdSeats reasoning corrected: the "no seat.hold() can fail midway / no stranded seats" claim now explicitly states it holds *provided every target seat is distinct*, and credits validateSeatRequest's duplicate rejection as the precondition that makes it true.
  - validateSeatRequest code snippet in the writeup updated to show the dedup logic with an explanatory comment.

TEST STATUS: mvn test -> Tests run: 14, Failures: 0, Errors: 0, Skipped: 0, BUILD SUCCESS (up from 13 before; the new test fails against the old code and passes with the fix).

Fixed the MAJOR deadlock-narrative defect by taking fix-option (b): changed the CODE so the retry happens OUTSIDE the spot lock, making the document's "one spot lock at a time / never grabs a second while holding it" claims actually true (instead of just rewriting the prose around a recursive-under-lock implementation).

CODE CHANGE — /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora_java/workspace/java-lld-parking-001_lld/src/main/java/com/interview/lld/parking/service/ParkingLotService.java (park(), formerly lines 93-120):
- Old: park() ran `return withSpotGuard(candidate, () -> { if (!candidate.isFree()) return park(vehicle); ... })` — the recursive retry executed WHILE STILL HOLDING candidate's lock, and the recursive call then acquired a DIFFERENT spot's lock, so a thread could transiently hold several spot locks.
- New: wrapped selection+guard in a `while (true)` loop. The guarded action now returns `null` as a sentinel when the re-check fails (instead of recursing); the guard releases candidate's lock on return, then the loop re-runs the strategy and acquires the next spot's lock. `openTickets.put(...)` moved outside the guard (registry is a ConcurrentHashMap; spot is already OCCUPIED under the lock, so it stays safe and shortens lock hold-time). ThreadSafeParkingLotService.withSpotGuard was untouched — only the base park() changed.

Empirically re-verified with the issue's own methodology (instrumented withSpotGuard subclass, 128 threads / 30k iterations churn): MAX simultaneous spot-locks held = 1 (was 4 before), lot ends empty (occupied=0, registry=0). So a thread now provably holds at most one spot lock and never grabs a second while holding the first — no lock-ordering cycle possible.

DOC CHANGES — /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora_java/workspace/java-lld-parking-001_lld/_response.md, made internally consistent with the new code:
- §5.5.B park pseudocode + dry-run table rewritten (loop outside guard, null sentinel, renumbered steps).
- §5.5.D tricky-logic "Fix in code" line updated (sentinel + retry outside lock).
- §6 park() code block + reasoning + spoken Q&A updated to the loop/sentinel form and the one-lock-at-a-time guarantee.
- §6 Verification Scenario 3 (race plain-English) updated.
- §9 Part B "exact modified method" code block updated to the loop form.
- §9 three flagged locations now accurate and true: the Deadlock row of the concurrency-vocabulary table; the "Why ReentrantLock" Q&A; the "What's the deadlock risk?" Q&A — each now explicitly attributes the one-lock-at-a-time property to the retry living OUTSIDE the guard. Also fixed §9 Step 2 narrative which still said "recurses".

§7 was NOT changed: re-ran Main (mvn exec:java) and stdout is byte-identical to the documented output (Cases 1-5 PASS, "ALL CASES PASSED"); mvn test still reports "Tests run: 15, Failures: 0, Errors: 0, Skipped: 0 / BUILD SUCCESS".

Constraints honored: Java 17+ idioms, single base exception unchanged, no `...` bodies, tests not weakened (fix was in production code). Final: mvn test green, 15/15 passing.

Fixed both MAJOR issues by strengthening the two concurrency tests so they actually exercise the per-customer lock and reliably catch its absence, plus minimal production seams to enable that. Final: `mvn test` = 12 tests, 0 failures, BUILD SUCCESS. `mvn exec:java` demo (§7.1) unchanged and still passes all 6 cases.

ROOT CAUSE: a real token bucket's critical section is a few ns of double arithmetic, so 50 barrier-released threads almost never truly overlap inside tryAcquire — the unguarded race almost never manifested (issue 2: caught only 2/10). And manyCustomers spawned ONE thread per customer, so no per-customer lock was ever contended (issue 1: caught 0/10).

PRODUCTION CHANGES (minimal, Template-Method-consistent):
- /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora_java/workspace/java-lld-ratelimiter-002_lld/src/main/java/com/interview/lld/ratelimiter/service/RateLimiterService.java — `newBucket()` changed `private` -> `protected` (a bucket-creation seam, like the existing acquireGate/releaseGate/resolveBucket hooks). Production never overrides it.
- /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora_java/workspace/java-lld-ratelimiter-002_lld/src/main/java/com/interview/lld/ratelimiter/service/ThreadSafeRateLimiterService.java — removed `final` so the test harness can extend it to combine the REAL per-customer lock with a widened critical section. `allow`, the gate hooks, and TokenBucketStrategy are all untouched.

TEST CHANGES (/Users/gbang/Downloads/algora_java/workspace/java-lld-ratelimiter-002_lld/src/test/java/com/interview/lld/ratelimiter/RateLimiterServiceTest.java):
- Added a test-only `WideWindowTokenBucket` strategy (integer tokens, frozen clock, classic lost-update race: read into local -> Thread.yield()+~50µs spin -> write back local-1). Injected via the new `newBucket()` seam through two test harnesses: `WideWindowThreadSafeService` (real lock) and `WideWindowBaseService` (lock-free). Under the lock the gap is a harmless wait; without it the race fires every run.
- Rewrote `concurrentBurstOnOneCustomerAllowsExactlyLimit` to run over the wide strategy (still 50 threads on a CyclicBarrier; strict allowed==limit, denied==threads-limit, errors==0).
- Rewrote `manyCustomersHaveNoCrossContention`: now 40 customers x 5 threads = 200 threads on ONE barrier so every customer's lock is genuinely contended; each customer must end with EXACTLY its limit.
- Added `baseServiceOverAllowsUnderWideRace` (the control: lock-free base over the same strategy MUST allowed > limit — locks in that the lock, not luck, bounds the count).
- Added `noisyCustomerNeverStealsAQuietCustomersBudget` (40 threads pound a noisy customer; a quiet customer keeps its full budget).

EMPIRICAL VALIDATION (same method the reviewer used): mutating the thread-safe harness to the lock-free base made the two main concurrency tests FAIL 10/10 runs (was 2/10 and 0/10 pre-fix). Restored, the suite is 12/12 green across 5 consecutive runs.

DOC: updated _response.md §7.2 (10->12 tests, wide-window rationale, 4 concurrency tests, mutation result), §9 Part C, the 30-second summary, and the §6 code snippets (newBucket now protected, ThreadSafe class no longer final) so the doc matches the source.
